prompt:
Our template-lint pipeline already parses HTML email templates into plain object trees, and every lint rule so far walks those trees by hand with bespoke recursion. I keep rejecting rule PRs because each one reinvents traversal slightly differently. Give the rules a selector engine instead: cssmatch.ts, zero dependencies, no DOM library, no jsdom/cheerio — the "document" is just our tree shape.

The tree shape (export the type):
  type DomNode = { tag: string; attrs: Record<string, string>; children: DomNode[] }
attrs is always an object (possibly empty), children always an array. Trees hold elements only — text is out of scope for matching. Tag names in our trees are lowercase; all comparisons (tags, class tokens, ids, attribute names and values) are exact and case-sensitive.

Exports:
- parseSelector(source: string) — parses one selector and returns your parsed representation (shape is your call; rules only need it to validate selectors up front). Anything outside the subset below throws a plain Error.
- select(root: DomNode, selector: string): DomNode[] — every element in the tree matching the selector, in pre-order document order, each node at most once. The root element itself participates in matching.

Supported selector subset — exactly this, nothing more:
- type (div), .class, #id, and compounds of them (div.card#main). Identifiers (tag names, class/id tokens, attribute names, unquoted values) are one or more of [A-Za-z0-9_-].
- .class matches when the element's class attribute, split on whitespace, contains the token ("items-list" must NOT match .item).
- [attr] — attribute present, even with an empty value. [attr=value] — whole-value equality; the value is either an unquoted identifier or a double-quoted string (may contain spaces, no escape sequences, runs to the next double quote). Attribute selectors are written tightly: no whitespace inside the brackets.
- :first-child and :nth-child(N) where N is a plain decimal integer >= 1, 1-based among the parent's children. The an+b forms (2n, odd, even) are NOT in scope — reject them. The root has no parent, so positional pseudo-classes never match it.
- Combinators: descendant (whitespace) and child (>, whitespace around it optional). Leading/trailing whitespace on the selector is fine.

Explicitly rejected with an Error (parse time, from both exports): the universal selector *, selector lists (commas), sibling combinators (+ and ~), pseudo-elements (::before etc.), any pseudo-class other than the two above, attribute operators other than plain = (no ^= $= *= ~=), single-quoted attribute values, dangling combinators, and empty selectors. Error messages are yours — the tests only require that an Error is thrown.

Matching semantics are the standard right-to-left ones: the rightmost compound matches the candidate element, a child combinator constrains the immediate parent, a descendant combinator matches ANY ancestor, and chains like `main section > p` must backtrack correctly when an inner compound can anchor at several ancestors. An element that matches through two different ancestors still appears once in the result.

Contract: node --test test_cssmatch.ts (node 26 runs TS by stripping types: plain typed TS only, no enums/namespaces, keep the ./cssmatch.ts import extension). The test file is read-only.
